antiPatterns: Repeatedly attempting the same density-dependent culling mechanism that has failed in the last three cycles.
Focusing on fox reproductive traits that don't account for energy scarcity.

directions.avoid: Traits that increase fox metabolic efficiency or stress resilience.
directions.label: A
directions.title: Fox Population Reproductive Constraint
directions.why: Direct density-dependent culling has failed. We must now target the reproductive resilience that allows the population to remain at 4777.

directions.avoid: Mechanisms that dump the entire buffer at once without consumption capacity.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Increase in active nutrients from the current 9.
directions.label: B
directions.title: Accelerate Nutrient Buffer Flux
directions.why: Active nutrients are insufficient for current biomass, and the buffer is not being converted efficiently.

directions.avoid: Traits that focus on beetle reproduction at the cost of survival against predators.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Sustainable growth of the beetle population.
directions.label: C
directions.title: Beetle Population Resilience
directions.why: The recovered beetle population must be protected to ensure it does not collapse again under fox pressure.

directions.avoid: Mechanisms that only benefit higher-trophic-level organisms.
directions.label: D
directions.title: Foundational Resource Dominance
directions.why: The fungus and root network must become more competitive for the energy currently dominated by foxes.

results.evidence: Nutrients increased from 6 to 9, but nutrient buffer remains at 100.
results.label: B

review.summary: Beetle recovery was a success (4 to 1303), but fox population reduction has not been achieved, and the nutrient buffer remains stagnant at 100 while active nutrients rose only slightly.

stateSignals: FOX population static at 4777 despite density-dependent culling attempts.
NutrientBuffer stagnant at 100, active nutrients only 9.
thesis: The ecosystem is thriving in beetle and fungal counts but remains structurally imbalanced due to an excessively high and unresponsive fox population. With beetle recovery secured, the immediate priority is to break the fox's dominance of the energy cycle. Previous attempts to cap the fox population have been inert; we must now shift strategies to either directly constrain fox reproductive resilience or shift resource allocation so aggressively toward foundational organisms (fungus/root network) that the fox population declines due to natural resource competition and scarcity.
